concepts.title: Biomechanics Definition
concepts.description: Biomechanics is the application of mechanical principles to living systems, particularly in the context of human movement and exercise.

concepts.title: Types of Movement
concepts.description: Movement is classified into three types: Flexion, Extension, and Rotation. Flexion is the movement of a part towards the midline of the body, Extension is the movement away from the midline, and Rotation is the movement around a central axis.

concepts.title: Range of Motion
concepts.description: Range of Motion (ROM) is the distance or amplitude of movement between the extremes of a joint's functional range. It is measured in terms of active ROM (AROM), passive ROM (PROM), and assisted ROM (AROM).

concepts.title: Foucault's Pendulum
concepts.description: Use the mnemonic 'Foucault's Pendulum Falls' to remember the three planes of movement: Sagittal, Frontal, and Transverse. Each word in the sentence corresponds to the first letter of each plane.

concepts.title: Misconception about Force
concepts.description: Students often confuse force with velocity. Force is a push or pull that causes an object to change its state of motion, while velocity is the rate of change of an object's position with respect to time.

concepts.title: Measurement Units
concepts.description: In biomechanics, it's essential to remember the measurement units for various parameters: Distance (meters, centimeters), Angle (degrees, radians), Velocity (meters per second), and Acceleration (meters per second squared).

concepts.title: Kinetic Chain
concepts.description: The kinetic chain refers to the interconnected sequence of joints and segments that transmit forces and movements throughout the body, from the ground up to the head and arms.

concepts.title: Types of Levers
concepts.description: Levers are classified into three types: First-class levers, Second-class levers, and Third-class levers. First-class levers have the fulcrum between the effort and the load, while Second-class levers have the effort between the fulcrum and the load, and Third-class levers have the load between the fulcrum and the effort.
